eventDefinitions.explanation: Windows logged a process crash. The important fields are the faulting application, faulting module, exception code, and fault offset.
Windows logged that the app stopped responding. Hangs are often caused by blocked IO, deadlocked threads, driver stalls, or overlays hooking the process.
Windows Error Reporting collected a crash or hang report. This often appears beside Event ID 1000 or 1002 for the same process.
A managed .NET app threw an unhandled exception. Check the message for the exception type and stack trace.
Windows Error Reporting recorded a system bug check report after a stop error.
The machine rebooted after a Windows stop error. The bug check code is usually the strongest first clue.
Windows started after it was not shut down cleanly. This can follow a BSOD, power loss, freeze, reset button press, or unstable overclock.
Windows noticed the previous shutdown was unexpected. Pair this with nearby BugCheck, Kernel-Power, disk, driver, or thermal events.
The display driver stopped responding and recovered. Repeated 4101 events near a game crash point toward GPU driver, GPU stability, or TDR issues.
Windows reported a bad block on a disk. Game files, shader caches, or paging activity on that disk can become unreliable.
Windows detected an IO warning during paging or disk access. Crashes around this event deserve a storage health check.
Windows Hardware Error Architecture logged a fatal hardware error. CPU, RAM, PCIe, GPU, BIOS, or voltage instability can be involved.
A Windows service failed to start. Around game launch this is especially useful for anti-cheat, launcher, driver, VPN, RGB, and update services.
A service could not start because a dependency failed. If a game launcher or anti-cheat service appears nearby, inspect the earlier dependency failure first.
A service terminated unexpectedly. Repeated service crashes near a game crash can explain launcher, anti-cheat, audio, network, or device-driver failures.
A service terminated unexpectedly without a clean recovery path. Check the service name and nearby events before blaming the foreground game.

exceptionCodes.name: STATUS_ACCESS_VIOLATION
exceptionCodes.explanation: The process tried to use memory it did not own. The faulting module shows where the bad access happened.

exceptionCodes.name: STATUS_HEAP_CORRUPTION
exceptionCodes.explanation: Windows found corrupted heap memory in this process. Something damaged memory before the crash was logged.

exceptionCodes.name: STATUS_STACK_BUFFER_OVERRUN
exceptionCodes.explanation: Windows stopped the process because stack memory was corrupted. Invalid code or a broken module likely ran inside the app.

exceptionCodes.name: STATUS_ILLEGAL_INSTRUCTION
exceptionCodes.explanation: The CPU hit an instruction it could not run. That can mean bad code in memory, a broken module, or a CPU feature mismatch.

exceptionCodes.name: .NET CLR exception
exceptionCodes.explanation: A .NET app threw an unhandled managed exception. The Application Error marks the exit, while the managed details live in the .NET Runtime event.

exceptionCodes.name: STATUS_STACK_OVERFLOW
exceptionCodes.explanation: This process ran out of thread stack space. That often means broken scripts, plugins, or a runaway loop inside the app.

exceptionCodes.name: STATUS_BREAKPOINT
exceptionCodes.explanation: The process hit a breakpoint exception. Debug tools, anti-tamper code, or a failed internal check can trigger this.

exceptionCodes.name: STATUS_DLL_NOT_FOUND
exceptionCodes.explanation: The process could not load a required DLL. The missing name in the event message is the important detail.

exceptionCodes.name: STATUS_DLL_INIT_FAILED
exceptionCodes.explanation: A DLL loaded but failed during startup. The faulting module usually points to the dependency that failed to initialize.

exceptionCodes.name: STATUS_INVALID_IMAGE_FORMAT
exceptionCodes.explanation: Windows tried to load a broken or mismatched binary image. This is a dependency format problem, not a gameplay crash.

bugChecks.name: VIDEO_TDR_FAILURE
bugChecks.explanation: The GPU stopped responding and Windows could not recover the display stack. This is a graphics driver or GPU stability failure.

bugChecks.name: PAGE_FAULT_IN_NONPAGED_AREA
bugChecks.explanation: Kernel code touched invalid memory that should have been resident. A driver, RAM, storage, or filter driver is usually involved.

bugChecks.name: SYSTEM_THREAD_EXCEPTION_NOT_HANDLED
bugChecks.explanation: A kernel system thread threw an unhandled exception. This usually points to a kernel driver named in the dump.

bugChecks.name: MEMORY_MANAGEMENT
bugChecks.explanation: Windows detected memory manager corruption. RAM instability or bad memory management is the likely cause.

bugChecks.name: CRITICAL_PROCESS_DIED
bugChecks.explanation: A process Windows treats as critical died. This is broader than a single game crash and can involve storage, drivers, or system files.

bugChecks.name: WHEA_UNCORRECTABLE_ERROR
bugChecks.explanation: Windows received an uncorrectable hardware error through WHEA. CPU, RAM, PCIe, GPU, storage, or power delivery can be involved.

bugChecks.name: DPC_WATCHDOG_VIOLATION
bugChecks.explanation: Windows detected stalled interrupt or DPC processing. A storage, network, USB, audio, GPU, or chipset driver is often involved.

bugChecks.name: VIDEO_SCHEDULER_INTERNAL_ERROR
bugChecks.explanation: The Windows video scheduler hit a fatal error. This is a graphics stack failure, not a generic app crash.

bugChecks.name: DRIVER_POWER_STATE_FAILURE
bugChecks.explanation: A driver got stuck during a power transition. Sleep, wake, USB, network, storage, Bluetooth, or GPU power paths are common sources.

bugChecks.name: SYSTEM_SERVICE_EXCEPTION
bugChecks.explanation: An exception happened while Windows was running a kernel service routine. The named driver in the dump is the key detail.

moduleHeuristics.explanation: The NVIDIA user-mode graphics driver was active when the process crashed.
The NVIDIA OpenGL driver was active when the process crashed.
The NVIDIA kernel display driver was involved in this failure.
An AMD DirectX user-mode driver was active when the process crashed.
An AMD DirectX driver module faulted during this crash.
An AMD OpenGL driver module was active when the process crashed.
An Intel graphics kernel driver was involved in this failure.
An Intel graphics compiler or runtime module appears in this crash signature.
The Direct3D 11 runtime was active when the process crashed.
DXGI was active during this crash. Swap chain, adapter, or presentation code was on the stack.
The Direct3D 12 runtime was active when the process crashed.
The DirectX graphics memory manager was involved in this failure.
The Windows watchdog driver was involved, often with a video scheduler or TDR failure.
Easy Anti-Cheat code was running when the process crashed.
BattlEye service code was running when the process crashed.
BattlEye client code was running when the process crashed.
BattlEye kernel driver code was involved in this failure.
EA AntiCheat code was running when the process crashed.
GameGuard anti-cheat code was running when the process crashed.
Riot Vanguard kernel driver code was involved in this failure.
Riot Vanguard user-mode service code was running when the process crashed.
The Universal C Runtime was on the crash stack. The app may have already been in a bad state before this call.
A Visual C++ runtime DLL was on the crash stack. The app may have already been in a bad state before this call.
Epic Online Services SDK code was running when the process crashed.
LWJGL native bridge code was active when the Java process crashed.
OpenAL audio code was active when the process crashed.
Unity engine code was active when the process crashed.
Unreal Engine code was active when the process crashed.
KERNELBASE is a common final reporting module. It is rarely the root cause by itself.
ntdll is deep Windows user-mode runtime code. It often appears at the end of a failure chain.
